Sixteen classification architectures from five design families were trained and assessed on the dataset to provide repeatable benchmark baselines. With an accuracy of 86.68%, precision of 88.65%, recall of 88.62%, and F1-score of 88.17%, ResNet101V2 performed the best overall. Per-class results showed balanced detection across all four defect categories within a narrow performance band of less than 1.2 percentage points. To promote open and repeatable research in automated PV inspection and solar energy operations and maintenance, the dataset, annotation files, and baseline code are made openly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SolarFCD, a unified multi-modal dataset of 4,435 solar panel images (RGB/Drone + Thermal Infrared) with four defect classes, benchmarks 16 classification architectures, and achieves best performance (86.68% accuracy) with ResNet101V2, releasing all resources openly.
AI.motivation: The lack of large-scale, multi-modal, publicly available annotated datasets is a major obstacle preventing advancement in automated solar PV inspection.
AI.method: Combined three public datasets across two imaging modalities (RGB/Drone and Thermal Infrared), performed label mapping, near-duplicate removal, minority class augmentation, 80:10:10 split, and benchmarked 16 classifiers from 5 design families.
AI.result: ResNet101V2 achieved best performance: 86.68% accuracy, 88.65% precision, 88.62% recall, 88.17% F1-score, with balanced detection across all four defect classes within 1.2 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: SolarFCD enables reproducible, open research in automated PV inspection and solar energy operations/maintenance by openly releasing dataset, annotations, and baseline code.

title: HAC: Parameter-Efficient Hyperbolic Adaptation of CLIP for Zero-Shot VQA
comment: This is the preprint version of the paper. The final version has been accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR 2026)
summary: Recent advances in representation learning have shown that hyperbolic geometry can offer a more expressive alternative to the Euclidean embeddings used in CLIP models, capturing hierarchical structures and leading to better-organized representations. However, current hyperbolic CLIP variants are trained entirely from scratch, which is computationally expensive and resource-intensive. In this work, we propose HAC (Hyperbolic Adaptation of CLIP), a parameter-efficient framework that enables pretrained CLIP models to transition into hyperbolic space via lightweight fine-tuning. We apply HAC to Visual Question Answering (VQA), where models must interpret visual elements and align them with textual queries. Notably, HAC's training is performed on a dataset with no overlap with any VQA benchmark, resulting in a strict zero-shot evaluation paradigm that underscores HAC's task-agnostic adaptability. We evaluate HAC across a diverse suite of VQA benchmarks spanning General, Reasoning, and OCR categories. Both HAC-S (small) and HAC-B (medium) consistently surpass Euclidean baselines and prior hyperbolic approaches, with HAC-B delivering up to a +1.9 point average improvement over CLIP-B on reasoning-intensive tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/fdibiton/HAC

AI.tldr: HAC proposes a parameter-efficient method to adapt pretrained CLIP models into hyperbolic space via lightweight fine-tuning, achieving better zero-shot VQA performance across multiple benchmarks without task-specific training data.
AI.motivation: Hyperbolic CLIP variants outperform Euclidean CLIP but require training from scratch, which is computationally expensive and resource-intensive. The authors aim to enable pretrained CLIP to transition into hyperbolic space efficiently.
AI.method: HAC (Hyperbolic Adaptation of CLIP) is a parameter-efficient framework that fine-tunes pretrained CLIP models into hyperbolic space. Training uses a dataset with no overlap with VQA benchmarks, enabling strict zero-shot evaluation.
AI.result: HAC-S and HAC-B consistently surpass Euclidean baselines and prior hyperbolic approaches. HAC-B achieves up to +1.9 point average improvement over CLIP-B on reasoning-intensive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic adaptation of pretrained CLIP is a computationally efficient and effective way to improve zero-shot VQA performance, demonstrating task-agnostic adaptability.

title: Deploy DINO with Many-to-Many Association
summary: Motivated by the limited generalization of supervised image matching models to unseen image domains, we explore the zero-shot deployment of DINO features for this task. The generalist visual representation extracted from DINO has inherent ambiguity when used to match feature points among semantically similar instances, prompting us to adopt a many-to-many (m-to-m) matching paradigm. However, the existing robust mechanism under m-to-m data association is computationally heavy, which requires finding a maximum-cardinality matching in the inlier association graph for each parameter evaluation. To address this inefficiency, we introduce a novel likelihood perspective, which interprets the existing method as a zeroth-order approximation of otherwise intractable likelihood calculation,and inspires us to propose a faster and finer-grained robust mechanism, termed as Harmonic Consensus Maximization (HCM). Take camera pose estimation as an exemplifying downstream task, we demonstrate that general-purpose visual features, used out of the box without any adaptation, can compete with specialized matching models on out-of-distribution datasets when mated with m-to-m association and the HCM mechanism.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Harmonic Consensus Maximization (HCM), a faster and finer-grained robust mechanism for many-to-many matching of DINO features in zero-shot image matching across domains, demonstrated on camera pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Supervised image matching models generalize poorly to unseen image domains. DINO features offer zero-shot potential but suffer from ambiguity when matching semantically similar instances, requiring a many-to-many matching paradigm that is computationally expensive.
AI.method: The authors reinterpret existing robust many-to-many matching as a zeroth-order approximation of likelihood calculation, and propose Harmonic Consensus Maximization (HCM) — a faster and finer-grained robust mechanism for many-to-many data association.
AI.result: General-purpose DINO features, used without any adaptation and combined with many-to-many association and HCM, can compete with specialized matching models on out-of-distribution datasets for camera pose estimation.
AI.conclusion: Many-to-many matching paired with the efficient HCM mechanism enables zero-shot deployment of DINO features to rival specialized models in cross-domain scenarios, solving the efficiency bottleneck in robust many-to-many association.

title: Learning to Decipher from Pixels -- A Case Study of Copiale
summary: Historical encrypted manuscripts require both paleographic interpretation of cipher symbols and cryptanalytic recovery of plaintext. Most existing computational workflows rely on a transcription-first paradigm, in which handwritten symbols are transcribed prior to decipherment. This intermediate step is labor-intensive, error-prone, and not always aligned with the goal of direct plaintext recovery. We propose an end-to-end, transcription-free approach that directly maps handwritten cipher images to plaintext. Using the Copiale cipher as a case study, we introduce the first text-line-level dataset pairing cipher images with German plaintext. We show that pretraining on generic handwriting data followed by cipher-specific fine-tuning substantially improves decipherment accuracy. Our results demonstrate that transcription-free image-to-plaintext decipherment is both feasible and effective for historical substitution ciphers, offering a simplified and scalable alternative to traditional pipelines. https://github.com/leitro/Decipher-from-Pixels-Copiale

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an end-to-end, transcription-free approach that directly maps handwritten cipher images to plaintext, using the Copiale cipher as a case study, achieving effective decipherment by pretraining on generic handwriting data followed by cipher-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Traditional computational decipherment workflows rely on a transcription-first paradigm where handwritten symbols must be manually transcribed before decipherment, which is labor-intensive, error-prone, and not aligned with direct plaintext recovery.
AI.method: The authors propose an end-to-end, transcription-free approach that directly maps handwritten cipher images to plaintext. They introduce the first text-line-level dataset pairing Copiale cipher images with German plaintext, and use pretraining on generic handwriting data followed by cipher-specific fine-tuning.
AI.result: The transcription-free image-to-plaintext decipherment is shown to be both feasible and effective, with pretraining on generic handwriting data followed by fine-tuning substantially improving decipherment accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a simplified and scalable alternative to traditional transcription-first pipelines for historical substitution ciphers.

title: Reading in the Dark: Low-light Scene Text Recognition
summary: Accurate text recognition in low-light environments is essential for intelligent systems in applications ranging from autonomous vehicles to smart surveillance. However, challenges such as poor illumination and noise interference remain underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce LSTR, a large-scale Low-light Scene Text Recognition dataset comprising 11,273 low-light images generated from well-lit datasets (ICDAR2015, IIIT5K, and WordArt), along with ESTR, which includes 60 real nighttime street-scene images in English and Spanish for exclusive evaluation. We explore two solution strategies: (1) employing Optical Character Recognition (OCR) models with fine-tuning and LoRA-based fine-tuning and (2) a joint training strategy that integrates a low-light image enhancement (LLIE) module with an OCR model. In particular, we propose a novel re-render LLIE (RLLIE) module, which demonstrates improved performance on real-world data. Through extensive experimentation, we analyze various training strategies and address a key research question: \emph{How bright is bright enough for effective scene text recognition?} Our results indicate that standalone LLIE or OCR models perform inadequately under low-light conditions, highlighting the advantages of specialized, jointly trained text-centric approaches. Additionally, we provide a comprehensive benchmark to support future research in robust low-light scene text recognition. https://huggingface.co/datasets/lumimusta/Low-light_Scene_Text_Dataset.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LSTR and ESTR datasets for low-light scene text recognition, proposes a novel re-render low-light image enhancement (RLLIE) module, and demonstrates that joint training of LLIE with OCR models outperforms standalone approaches.
AI.motivation: Low-light environment text recognition is critical for intelligent systems (autonomous vehicles, surveillance), yet challenges like poor illumination and noise remain underexplored. No large-scale benchmark existed for this problem.
AI.method: Created LSTR (11,273 low-light images from existing datasets) and ESTR (60 real nighttime images). Explored two strategies: (1) OCR with fine-tuning/LoRA, (2) joint training of LLIE + OCR. Proposed the RLLIE module for improved real-world performance.
AI.result: Standalone LLIE or OCR models perform inadequately under low-light conditions. The proposed joint training approach with RLLIE module shows superior performance, especially on real-world data.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, jointly trained text-centric approaches are advantageous for low-light scene text recognition. The paper provides benchmarks and datasets to support future research.

title: Do Protective Perturbations Really Protect Portrait Privacy under Real-world Image Transformations?
summary: Proactive defense methods protect portrait images from unauthorized editing or talking face generation (TFG) by introducing pixel-level protective perturbations, and have already attracted increasing attention for privacy protection. In real-world scenarios, images inevitably undergo various transformations during cross-device display and dissemination--such as scale transformations and color compression--that directly alter pixel values. However, it remains unclear whether such pixel-level modifications affect the effectiveness of existing proactive defense methods that rely on pixel-level perturbations. To solve this problem, we conduct a systematic evaluation of representative proactive defenses under image transformation. The evaluated methods are selected to span different generation architectures such as diffusion and GAN-based models, as well as defense scopes covering both portrait and natural images, and are assessed using both qualitative and quantitative metrics for subjective and objective comparison. Experimental results indicate that defense methods based on pixel-level perturbations struggle to withstand common image transformations, posing a risk of defense failure in real-world applications. To further highlight this risk, we propose a simple yet effective purification framework by leveraging the vulnerabilities induced by real-world image transformations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can efficiently remove protective perturbations with low computational cost, highlighting previously overlooked risks to the research community.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates existing proactive defense methods for portrait privacy under real-world image transformations (scaling, compression) and finds they fail; proposes a simple purification framework to remove protective perturbations efficiently.
AI.motivation: Real-world image transformations (scale, color compression) alter pixel values, and it is unclear whether existing pixel-level proactive defense methods remain effective under such conditions—posing a risk to privacy protection.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of representative proactive defenses (spanning diffusion & GAN-based models, portrait & natural images) under common image transformations; proposes a purification framework exploiting vulnerabilities from transformations.
AI.result: Defense methods based on pixel-level perturbations fail under common image transformations; the proposed purification framework efficiently removes protective perturbations with low computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Existing proactive defenses are vulnerable to real-world image transformations, highlighting previously overlooked risks; the proposed purification framework demonstrates the severity of this vulnerability.

title: A Pose-only Geometric Constraint for Multi-Camera Pose Adjustment
summary: Multi-camera systems offer rich observation capabilities for visual navigation and 3D scene reconstruction; however, the resulting feature redundancy often compromises computational efficiency. This challenge is particularly pronounced during bundle adjustment, where the non-linear optimization of both system poses and scene points incurs substantial computational overhead. To address this challenge, this paper introduces a pose-only geometric constraint for multi-camera systems and proposes a corresponding pose adjustment algorithm. Specifically, we use generalized camera model to establish a unified representation of the multi-camera system. Building upon this model, we formulate the multi-camera pose-only constraint, which implicitly represents a 3D scene point using two base observations and their associated poses, thereby achieving a pose-only representation of the projection geometry. Subsequently, we introduce a multi-camera pose adjustment algorithm that eliminates 3D points from the parameter space, thereby achieving efficient and focused pose optimization. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms baseline bundle adjustment methods in computational efficiency, while maintaining or even improving pose estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a pose-only geometric constraint and adjustment algorithm for multi-camera systems that eliminates 3D points from the optimization parameter space, improving computational efficiency in bundle adjustment while maintaining or improving pose estimation accuracy.
AI.motivation: Multi-camera systems suffer from feature redundancy that compromises computational efficiency, especially during bundle adjustment where optimizing both poses and 3D scene points incurs substantial computational overhead.
AI.method: Uses generalized camera model for unified representation, formulates a multi-camera pose-only constraint that implicitly represents 3D points using two base observations and associated poses, then introduces a pose adjustment algorithm that eliminates 3D points from parameter space.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show the proposed algorithm outperforms baseline bundle adjustment methods in computational efficiency while maintaining or improving pose estimation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pose-only approach effectively addresses the computational inefficiency of bundle adjustment in multi-camera systems by removing 3D points from optimization, achieving faster and equally or more accurate pose estimation.

title: Weakly Supervised Multicenter Nancy Index Scoring in Ulcerative Colitis Using Foundation Models
summary:
Histologic assessment of ulcerative colitis (UC) activity is an important endpoint in clinical trials and routine care, but manual grading with indices such as the Nancy histological index (NHI) is time-consuming and prone to observer variability. While computational pathology methods can automate scoring, many approaches depend on dense region-level annotations, which are costly to obtain, particularly in heterogeneous, multicenter cohorts.
  We propose a weakly supervised multiple instance learning (MIL) approach for whole-slide images that learns from case- and slide-level NHI labels, leveraging foundation models. Our method targets clinically relevant endpoints, including neutrophilic activity and derived Nancy-low/high groupings, enabling full five-grade NHI prediction.
  On a multicenter dataset of H&E-stained colon biopsies from three hospitals (2019-2025), we evaluate multiple foundation model encoders and aggregation strategies. We find that foundation model choice and resolution substantially affect performance, with Virchow2 providing the most consistent gains, and that a simple ensembling rule improves five-grade NHI prediction compared to a hierarchical gating baseline.
  Overall, our results demonstrate that weakly supervised MIL with modern foundation-model representations can provide robust, interpretable UC histology activity assessment in realistic multicenter settings.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a weakly supervised multiple instance learning (MIL) approach using foundation models to automatically grade ulcerative colitis histology activity (Nancy histological index) from whole-slide images, achieving robust performance on a multicenter dataset without requiring costly dense annotations.
AI.motivation: Histologic assessment of UC activity is important but manual grading (e.g., Nancy histological index) is time-consuming and prone to observer variability. Existing computational methods require costly dense region-level annotations, which are impractical for heterogeneous multicenter cohorts.
AI.method: A weakly supervised multiple instance learning (MIL) approach for whole-slide images, trained on case- and slide-level NHI labels, leveraging foundation models (e.g., Virchow2). Multiple encoder and aggregation strategies are evaluated.
AI.result: Foundation model choice and resolution substantially affect performance, with Virchow2 providing the most consistent gains. A simple ensembling rule improves five-grade NHI prediction over a hierarchical gating baseline.
AI.conclusion: Weakly supervised MIL with modern foundation-model representations can provide robust, interpretable UC histology activity assessment in realistic multicenter settings without dense annotations.

title: ZID-Net: Zero-Inference Diffusion Prior Decoupling Network for Single Image Dehazing
comment: Submitted to Neurocomputing. Includes 12 figures and 8 tables
summary: Single image dehazing is often constrained by a trade-off between restoration quality and computational efficiency. While efficient, CNN networks struggle to learn robust priors for dense and non-homogeneous haze. Conversely, diffusion models provide strong generative priors but suffer from severe inference latency and sampling instability. To address these limitations, we propose ZID-Net, a novel framework that explicitly decouples diffusion supervision from feed-forward inference. For efficient inference, we design a frequency-spatial decoupled feed-forward backbone. Within this backbone, a Channel-Spatial Laplacian Mask (CSLM) filters haze-amplified noise to extract purified structural details, while Lightweight Global Context Blocks (LGCBs) establish long-range spatial dependencies to capture the global variations of haze. A Dynamic Feature Arbitration Block (DFAB) then adaptively fuses these semantic and structural features for robust reconstruction. To provide this backbone with physical priors without the inference cost, we introduce a Zero-Inference Prior Propagation Head (ZI-PPH) during training. ZI-PPH leverages a conditional diffusion process to predict residual noise, providing degradation-aware structural supervision to the backbone. By discarding the diffusion branch at test time, ZID-Net integrates diffusion priors into a pure feed-forward architecture for accurate and efficient restoration. ZID-Net achieves 40.75 dB PSNR on the synthetic RESIDE dataset and outperforms existing methods with a 1.13 dB gain on real-world datasets. Additionally, it yields a 3.06 dB PSNR gain on the StateHaze1k remote sensing dataset with an inference time of just 19.35 ms. The project code is available at: https://github.com/XoomitLXH/ZID-Net.

AI.tldr: ZID-Net is a single image dehazing framework that decouples diffusion supervision from feed-forward inference, using a frequency-spatial decoupled backbone with CSLM, LGCB, and DFAB modules for efficient inference, and a Zero-Inference Prior Propagation Head (ZI-PPH) that leverages diffusion priors during training only. It achieves 40.75 dB PSNR on RESIDE, outperforms existing methods by 1.13 dB on real-world datasets, and runs in 19.35 ms.
AI.motivation: Existing dehazing methods face a trade-off: CNNs are efficient but struggle with dense/non-homogeneous haze, while diffusion models offer strong priors but suffer from high inference latency and sampling instability. ZID-Net aims to combine the best of both worlds.
AI.method: ZID-Net decouples diffusion supervision from feed-forward inference. It uses a frequency-spatial decoupled feed-forward backbone with: (1) Channel-Spatial Laplacian Mask (CSLM) to filter haze-amplified noise and extract structural details, (2) Lightweight Global Context Blocks (LGCBs) for long-range spatial dependencies, and (3) Dynamic Feature Arbitration Block (DFAB) for adaptive fusion. During training, a Zero-Inference Prior Propagation Head (ZI-PPH) uses a conditional diffusion process to predict residual noise and provide degradation-aware supervision, but is discarded at test time.
AI.result: ZID-Net achieves 40.75 dB PSNR on the synthetic RESIDE dataset, outperforms existing methods with a 1.13 dB gain on real-world datasets, and delivers a 3.06 dB PSNR gain on the StateHaze1k remote sensing dataset with an inference time of only 19.35 ms.
AI.conclusion: By integrating diffusion priors into a pure feed-forward architecture, ZID-Net achieves both accurate and efficient image dehazing, effectively resolving the trade-off between restoration quality and computational efficiency.

title: Caries DETR: Tooth Structure-aware Prior and Lesion-aware Dynamic Loss Refinement for DETR Based Caries Detection
summary: As dental caries appear as subtle, low-contrast lesions in intraoral imaging, existing deep learning models face significant challenges in the early detection of caries. While recent Transformer-based detectors have shown promising results in natural images, they often fail to capture the domain-specific anatomical priors crucial for dental caries detection. In this paper, we propose Caries-DETR, a specialized Transformer framework for caries detection in intraoral images. A Tooth Structure-aware Query Initialization (TSQI) is designed, leveraging large-scale intraoral photograph pre-training and a structure perception branch (SPB) to integrate high-frequency structural priors, guiding the model to focus on anatomically significant lesion areas. Furthermore, we design a Lesion-aware Dynamic Loss Refinement (LDLR) to implement quality-driven hard mining through adaptive loss reweighting based on lesion size, anatomical relevance, and prediction quality, optimizing detection for subtle lesions. Extensive experiments on two public datasets (i.e., AlphaDent and DentalAI) demonstrate that Caries-DETR achieves a state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods and exhibits good generalization and robustness. Code and data at https://github.com/XuefenLiu-SZU/Caries-DETR}{https://github.com/XuefenLiu-SZU/Caries-DETR.

AI.tldr: Caries-DETR is a specialized Transformer framework for early dental caries detection in intraoral images, featuring Tooth Structure-aware Query Initialization (TSQI) and Lesion-aware Dynamic Loss Refinement (LDLR), achieving state-of-the-art results on AlphaDent and DentalAI datasets.
AI.motivation: Dental caries appear as subtle, low-contrast lesions in intraoral imaging, making early detection challenging for existing deep learning models. Transformer-based detectors fail to capture domain-specific anatomical priors crucial for caries detection.
AI.method: (1) Tooth Structure-aware Query Initialization (TSQI): uses large-scale intraoral photograph pre-training and a structure perception branch (SPB) to integrate high-frequency structural priors. (2) Lesion-aware Dynamic Loss Refinement (LDLR): implements quality-driven hard mining via adaptive loss reweighting based on lesion size, anatomical relevance, and prediction quality.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on two public datasets (AlphaDent and DentalAI), with good generalization and robustness compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Caries-DETR effectively addresses the challenge of early caries detection by incorporating anatomical priors and adaptive loss refinement, outperforming existing approaches.

title: Zoom In, Reason Out: Efficient Far-field Anomaly Detection in Expressway Surveillance Videos via Focused VLM Reasoning Guided by Bayesian Inference
summary: Expressway video anomaly detection is essential for safety management. However, identifying anomalies across diverse scenes remains challenging, particularly for far-field targets exhibiting subtle abnormal vehicle motions. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate strong semantic reasoning capabilities, processing global frames causes attention dilution for these far-field objects and incurs prohibitive computational costs. To address these issues, we propose VIBES, an asynchronous collaborative framework utilizing VLMs guided by Bayesian inference. Specifically, to overcome poor generalization across varying expressway environments, we introduce an online Bayesian inference module. This module continuously evaluates vehicle trajectories to dynamically update the probabilistic boundaries of normal driving behaviors, serving as an asynchronous trigger to precisely localize anomalies in space and time. Instead of processing the continuous video stream, the VLM processes only the localized visual regions indicated by the trigger. This targeted visual input prevents attention dilution and enables accurate semantic reasoning. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that VIBES improves detection accuracy for far-field anomalies and reduces computational overhead, achieving high real-time efficiency and explainability while demonstrating generalization across diverse expressway conditions.

AI.tldr: VIBES is an asynchronous collaborative framework that combines Bayesian inference for online trajectory evaluation with Vision-Language Models for targeted anomaly detection, improving far-field anomaly detection accuracy and computational efficiency on expressways.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-based methods for expressway anomaly detection suffer from attention dilution on far-field objects and high computational costs when processing global frames, while also struggling with generalization across diverse expressway environments.
AI.method: Proposes an asynchronous framework where an online Bayesian inference module continuously evaluates vehicle trajectories to update probabilistic boundaries of normal behaviors, triggering the VLM to process only localized visual regions where anomalies are spatially and temporally indicated.
AI.result: VIBES improves detection accuracy for far-field anomalies, reduces computational overhead, achieves high real-time efficiency and explainability, and generalizes across diverse expressway conditions.
AI.conclusion: The asynchronous collaboration between Bayesian inference and VLMs effectively addresses attention dilution and computational cost issues, enabling practical and explainable anomaly detection in expressway scenarios.

title: ESIA: An Energy-Based Spatiotemporal Interaction-Aware Framework for Pedestrian Intention Prediction
summary: Recent advances in autonomous driving have motivated research on pedestrian intention prediction, which aims to infer future crossing decisions and actions by modeling temporal dynamics, social interactions, and environmental context. However, existing studies remain constrained by oversimplified multi-agent interaction patterns, opaque reasoning logic, and a lack of global consistency in behavioral predictions, which compromise both robustness and interpretability. In this work, we propose ESIA (Energy-based Spatiotemporal Interaction-Aware framework), a novel Conditional Random Field (CRF)-based paradigm. We cast the intention prediction task as a structured prediction problem over a unified graph-based representation, treating pedestrians and the environment as spatiotemporal nodes. To characterize their distinct roles, we assign unary potentials to nodes to capture individual intentions, and pairwise potentials to edges to encode social and environmental interactions. These potentials are integrated into a unified global energy function to ensure scene-level consistency across behavioral predictions. To further constrain inference without ground-truth supervision, we introduce structural consistency terms to penalize logical contradictions. This optimization is efficiently solved via a novel Unary-Seeded Simulated Annealing (U-SSA) algorithm, which leverages high-confidence unary priors to rapidly converge to a high-quality solution. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that ESIA achieves state-of-the-art performance with improved interpretability over existing methods.

AI.tldr: ESIA proposes a CRF-based framework for pedestrian intention prediction that models spatiotemporal interactions via unary and pairwise potentials, optimizes with a Unary-Seeded Simulated Annealing algorithm, and achieves SOTA performance with better interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing pedestrian intention prediction works suffer from oversimplified multi-agent interactions, opaque reasoning logic, and lack of global consistency in behavioral predictions, harming robustness and interpretability.
AI.method: ESIA formulates intention prediction as a structured CRF problem over a graph, using unary potentials for individual intentions and pairwise potentials for social/environmental interactions, integrated into a global energy function. Structural consistency terms penalize logical contradictions. Inference is solved via a novel Unary-Seeded Simulated Annealing (U-SSA) algorithm that leverages high-confidence priors for rapid convergence.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show ESIA achieves state-of-the-art performance with improved interpretability over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: ESIA effectively addresses the limitations of prior work by introducing a structured, energy-based CRF framework with interpretable reasoning and global scene-level consistency, validated by SOTA results.

title: DynProto: Dynamic Prototype Evolution for Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Recent studies show that using potential out-of-distribution (OOD) labels from large corpora as auxiliary information can improve OOD detection in vision-language models (VLMs). However, these methods often fail when real-world OOD samples fall outside the predefined OOD label set. To address this limitation, we propose DynProto, a novel approach that learns OOD prototypes dynamically during testing using only in-distribution (ID) information. DynProto is inspired by a key observation: OOD samples predicted as the same ID class tend to cluster in the feature space. With this insight, we leverage easy-to-detect OOD samples as ``anchors'' to find their harder-to-detect, similar counterparts. To this end, DynProto introduces two modules: \textbf{Coarse OOD Pattern Capturing Module} caches OOD patterns that are easily confused with each ID class during testing, and \textbf{Fine-grained OOD Pattern Refinement Module} subsequently clusters these patterns within each cache and aggregates them into representative OOD prototypes. By measuring similarity to ID and dynamic OOD prototypes, DynProto enables accurate OOD detection. DynProto significantly outperforms prior methods across multiple benchmarks. On ImageNet OOD benchmark, DynProto reduces FPR95 by 11.60\% and improves AUROC by 4.70\%. Moreover, the framework is architecture-agnostic and can be integrated into various backbones.

AI.tldr: DynProto dynamically learns OOD prototypes during testing using only ID information, achieving state-of-the-art OOD detection for VLMs without needing predefined OOD labels.
AI.motivation: Existing OOD detection methods that use auxiliary OOD labels from large corpora fail when real-world OOD samples fall outside the predefined label set. The authors address this limitation by learning OOD prototypes dynamically without requiring predefined OOD labels.
AI.method: DynProto consists of two modules: (1) Coarse OOD Pattern Capturing Module - caches OOD patterns easily confused with each ID class during testing; (2) Fine-grained OOD Pattern Refinement Module - clusters these patterns within each cache and aggregates them into representative OOD prototypes. OOD detection is performed by measuring similarity to both ID prototypes and dynamic OOD prototypes.
AI.result: On ImageNet OOD benchmark, DynProto reduces FPR95 by 11.60% and improves AUROC by 4.70% over prior methods. It significantly outperforms prior methods across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DynProto is a novel, architecture-agnostic framework that enables accurate OOD detection without relying on predefined OOD labels, and can be integrated into various backbone architectures.

title: Edit Where You Mean: Region-Aware Adapter Injection for Mask-Free Local Image Editing
summary: Large diffusion transformers (DiTs) follow global editing instructions well but consistently leak local edits into unrelated regions, because joint-attention architectures offer no explicit channel telling the network where to apply the edit. We introduce REDEdit, a co-trained, instruction- and region-aware adapter framework that retrofits a frozen DiT into a precise local editor without modifying its backbone weights. A lightweight Block Adapter at every transformer block injects a structured condition stream that factorizes what to edit (instruction semantics) from where to edit (spatial mask); a learned SpatialGate routes the adapter signal selectively into the edit region while keeping the rest of the image near-identical to the source; and a Region-Aware Loss focuses the training objective on the changing pixels. Because these components make the backbone's internal representation mask-aware end-to-end, a thin MaskPredictor head trained jointly with the editor can ground the edit region directly from the instruction and source image eliminating any user-mask requirement at deployment. We evaluate on two complementary benchmarks: MagicBrush (paired ground-truth targets) to measure pixel-level preservation and edit accuracy, and Emu-Edit Test (no ground-truth images, 9 diverse edit categories) to stress-test instruction following and generalization across edit types. On both, REDEdit achieves state-of-the-art results, simultaneously outperforming mask-free and oracle-mask baselines. A seven-variant ablation cleanly isolates the contribution of each component.

AI.tldr: REDEdit is a lightweight adapter framework for frozen Diffusion Transformers that enables precise local image editing by factorizing edit instructions from spatial masks, using a SpatialGate for region-selective signal routing, a Region-Aware Loss, and an optional MaskPredictor that removes user mask requirements, achieving state-of-the-art results on MagicBrush and Emu-Edit Test benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Large diffusion transformers (DiTs) with joint-attention architectures cannot locally constrain edits—they leak changes into unrelated regions because there is no explicit mechanism to tell the network where to apply edits.
AI.method: REDEdit uses (1) Block Adapters at each transformer block to inject structured condition streams separating instruction semantics from spatial masks; (2) a learned SpatialGate to route adapter signals only into edit regions; (3) Region-Aware Loss focused on changing pixels; and (4) a MaskPredictor head trained jointly to predict edit regions from instructions alone, removing user mask requirements at deployment.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on MagicBrush (pixel-level preservation and edit accuracy) and Emu-Edit Test (9 diverse edit categories), outperforming both mask-free and oracle-mask baselines. A seven-variant ablation validates each component's contribution.
AI.conclusion: REDEdit effectively retrofits frozen DiTs into precise local editors without backbone modifications, factorizes what-to-edit from where-to-edit, and can optionally eliminate user mask requirements, setting new SOTA on two complementary benchmarks.

title: From Noisy Historical Maps to Time-Series Oil Palm Mapping Without Annotation in Malaysia and Indonesia (2020-2024)
summary: Accurate monitoring of oil palm plantations is critical for balancing economic development with environmental conservation in Southeast Asia. However, existing plantation maps often suffer from low spatial resolution and a lack of recent temporal coverage, impeding effective surveillance of rapid land-use changes. In this study, we propose a deep learning framework to generate 10-meter resolution oil palm plantation maps for Indonesia and Malaysia from 2020 to 2024, utilizing Sentinel-2 imagery without requiring new manual annotations. To address the resolution mismatch between coarse 100-meter historical labels and 10-meter imagery, we employ a U-Net architecture optimized with Determinant-based Mutual Information (DMI). This approach effectively mitigates the influence of label noise. We validated our method against 2,058 manually verified points, achieving overall accuracies of 70.64%, 63.53%, and 60.06% for the years 2020, 2022, and 2024, respectively. Our comprehensive analysis reveals that oil palm coverage in the region peaked in 2022 before experiencing a decline in 2024. Furthermore, land cover transition analysis highlights a concerning trajectory of plantation expansion into flooded vegetation areas, despite a general stabilization in rotations with other crop types. These high-resolution maps provide essential data for monitoring sustainability commitments and deforestation dynamics in the region, and the generated datasets are made publicly available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17768444.

AI.tldr: This study proposes a deep learning framework using U-Net with DMI loss to generate 10-meter resolution oil palm plantation maps for Indonesia and Malaysia (2020-2024) from Sentinel-2 imagery without new manual annotations, achieving 60-70% accuracy and revealing plantation expansion into flooded vegetation areas.
AI.motivation: Existing oil palm plantation maps suffer from low spatial resolution and outdated temporal coverage, hindering effective monitoring of rapid land-use changes in Southeast Asia where balancing economic development with environmental conservation is critical.
AI.method: A U-Net architecture optimized with Determinant-based Mutual Information (DMI) loss to handle resolution mismatch between coarse 100-meter historical labels and 10-meter Sentinel-2 imagery, requiring no new manual annotations.
AI.result: Validation against 2,058 manually verified points showed overall accuracies of 70.64% (2020), 63.53% (2022), and 60.06% (2024). Oil palm coverage peaked in 2022 then declined in 2024, with concerning expansion into flooded vegetation areas.
AI.conclusion: The high-resolution maps provide essential data for monitoring sustainability commitments and deforestation dynamics, with datasets made publicly available.

title: ClawMark: A Living-World Benchmark for Multi-Turn, Multi-Day, Multimodal Coworker Agents
summary: Language-model agents are increasingly used as persistent coworkers that assist users across multiple working days. During such workflows, the surrounding environment may change independently of the agent: new emails arrive, calendar entries shift, knowledge-base records are updated, and evidence appears across images, scanned PDFs, audio, video, and spreadsheets. Existing benchmarks do not adequately evaluate this setting because they typically run within a single static episode and remain largely text-centric. We introduce \bench{}, a benchmark for coworker agents built around multi-turn multi-day tasks, a stateful sandboxed service environment whose state evolves between turns, and rule-based verification. The current release contains 100 tasks across 13 professional scenarios, executed against five stateful sandboxed services (filesystem, email, calendar, knowledge base, spreadsheet) and scored by 1537 deterministic Python checkers over post-execution service state; no LLM-as-judge is invoked during scoring. We benchmark seven frontier agent systems. The strongest model reaches 75.8 weighted score, but the best strict Task Success is only 20.0\%, indicating that partial progress is common while complete end-to-end workflow completion remains rare. Turn-level analysis shows that performance drops after the first exogenous environment update, highlighting adaptation to changing state as a key open challenge. We release the benchmark, evaluation harness, and construction pipeline to support reproducible coworker-agent evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new benchmark (COWORKER) for evaluating language-model agents as persistent coworkers across multi-day workflows with evolving environments (emails, calendars, files, etc.). The benchmark includes 100 tasks across 13 scenarios, sandboxed services, and rule-based scoring. The best agent achieves 75.8 weighted score but only 20.0% full task success, highlighting adaptation challenges.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate agents in static, single-episode, text-centric settings, which inadequately represent real-world coworker scenarios where agents operate across multiple days and must adapt to autonomously changing environments (e.g., new emails, updated records, multimedia evidence).
AI.method: The authors build a benchmark around multi-turn multi-day tasks with a stateful sandboxed service environment (filesystem, email, calendar, knowledge base, spreadsheet) whose state evolves between turns. Tasks span 13 professional scenarios, scored by 1537 deterministic Python checkers on post-execution service state without LLM-as-judge.
AI.result: Seven frontier agent systems were evaluated. The strongest model reached 75.8 weighted score, but strict Task Success was only 20.0%, indicating partial progress is common. Turn-level analysis shows performance drops after the first exogenous environment update, revealing adaptation as a key challenge.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark reveals that current LLM agents struggle with multi-day workflows requiring adaptation to changing environments. The authors release the benchmark, evaluation harness, and pipeline to support further research in coworker-agent evaluation.

title: MIRAGE: A Micro-Interaction Relational Architecture for Grounded Exploration in Multi-Figure Artworks
comment: 14 pages (11 pages main text), 6 figures, 1 table
summary:
Appreciating multi-figure paintings requires understanding how characters relate through subtle cues like gaze alignment, gesture, and spatial arrangement. We present MIRAGE, an evidence-centric framework designed to scaffold the exploration of these "micro-interactions" in multi-figure artworks. While such cues are essential for deep narrative appreciation, they are often distributed across complex scenes and difficult for viewers to systematically identify. Existing vision-language models (VLMs) frequently fail to provide reliable assistance, offering ungrounded interpretations that lack traceable visual evidence.
  MIRAGE addresses this by constructing a structured intermediate representation capturing identities, pose cues, and gaze hypotheses. However, the challenge extends beyond extracting these cues to coordinating them during interpretation. Without an explicit mechanism to organize and reconcile relational evidence, models often collapse multiple interaction hypotheses into a single unstable or weakly grounded narrative, even when low-level signals are available. This representation allows users to verify how high-level interpretations are anchored in low-level visual facts.
  By separating spatial grounding from narrative generation, MIRAGE enables users to inspect and reason about figure-to-figure relationships through a verifiable evidence layer. We evaluate MIRAGE against painting-only VLM baselines using a blind assessment protocol. Results show that MIRAGE significantly improves identity consistency, reduces relational hallucinations, and increases the coverage of subtle interactions. These findings suggest that structured grounding can serve as a critical interaction control layer, providing the necessary scaffolding for a more reliable, transparent, and human-led understanding of complex visual narratives.

AI.tldr: MIRAGE is a framework that builds structured, verifiable evidence layers (identities, poses, gaze) for multi-figure paintings, improving VLM reliability by separating spatial grounding from narrative generation, reducing hallucinations and improving interaction coverage.
AI.motivation: Multi-figure paintings contain subtle micro-interactions (gaze, gesture, spatial cues) that are critical for narrative understanding but are distributed and hard to systematically identify. Existing VLMs provide ungrounded interpretations lacking traceable visual evidence, so a structured, verifiable approach is needed.
AI.method: MIRAGE constructs a structured intermediate representation capturing identities, pose cues, and gaze hypotheses, then separates spatial grounding from narrative generation to allow users to inspect how high-level interpretations anchor to low-level visual facts.
AI.result: Compared to painting-only VLM baselines under blind assessment, MIRAGE significantly improves identity consistency, reduces relational hallucinations, and increases coverage of subtle interactions.
AI.conclusion: Structured grounding serves as a critical interaction control layer, providing scaffolding for more reliable, transparent, and human-led understanding of complex visual narratives in multi-figure artworks.

title: MuSS: A Large-Scale Dataset and Cinematic Narrative Benchmark for Multi-Shot Subject-to-Video Generation
summary: While video foundation models excel at single-shot generation, real-world cinematic storytelling inherently relies on complex multi-shot sequencing. Further progress is constrained by the absence of datasets that address three core challenges: authentic narrative logic, spatiotemporal text-video alignment conflicts, and the "copy-paste" dilemma prevalent in Subject-to-Video (S2V) generation. To bridge this gap, we introduce MuSS, a large-scale, dual-track dataset tailored for multi-shot video and S2V generation. Sourced from over 3,000 movies, MuSS explicitly supports both complex montage transitions and subject-centric narratives. To construct this dataset, we pioneer a progressive captioning pipeline that eliminates contextual conflicts by ensuring local shot-level accuracy before enforcing global narrative coherence. Crucially, we implement a cross-shot matching mechanism to fundamentally eradicate the S2V copy-paste shortcut. Alongside the dataset, we propose the Cinematic Narrative Benchmark, featuring a visual-logic-driven paradigm and a novel Anti-Copy-Paste Variance (ACP-Var) metric to rigorously assess continuous storytelling and 3D structural consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that while current baselines struggle with continuous narrative logic or degenerate into trivial 2D sticker generators, our MuSS-augmented model achieves state-of-the-art narrative effectiveness and cross-shot identity preservation.

AI.tldr: MuSS is a large-scale, movie-sourced dataset for multi-shot video and Subject-to-Video generation, featuring a progressive captioning pipeline, cross-shot matching to prevent copy-paste shortcuts, and a Cinematic Narrative Benchmark with a novel ACP-Var metric.
AI.motivation: Current video foundation models excel at single-shot generation but fail at multi-shot cinematic storytelling due to three key challenges: lack of authentic narrative logic, spatiotemporal text-video alignment conflicts, and the copy-paste dilemma in Subject-to-Video generation. No existing dataset addresses all three issues.
AI.method: The authors built MuSS from 3,000+ movies, designed a progressive captioning pipeline (local shot accuracy → global coherence), implemented cross-shot matching to eliminate S2V copy-paste shortcuts, and proposed the Cinematic Narrative Benchmark with a visual-logic paradigm and ACP-Var metric for 3D structural consistency evaluation.
AI.result: Current baselines struggle with narrative logic or degenerate into 2D sticker generators, while the MuSS-augmented model achieves state-of-the-art narrative effectiveness and cross-shot identity preservation.
AI.conclusion: MuSS provides a crucial foundation for advancing multi-shot video generation, addressing dataset gaps in narrative logic, alignment, and identity consistency, and enabling models to move beyond single-shot generation toward real cinematic storytelling.

title: VitaminP: cross-modal learning enables whole-cell segmentation from routine histology
comment: 44 pages, 10 figures. Code and models available
summary: Accurate whole-cell and nuclear segmentation is essential for precision pathology and spatial omics, yet routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining provides limited cytoplasmic contrast, restricting analyses to nuclei. Multiplex immunofluorescence (mIF) facilitates precise whole-cell delineation but remains constrained by cost and accessibility. We introduce VitaminP, a cross-modal learning framework enabling whole cell segmentation from H&E images. By learning from paired H&E-mIF data, VitaminP transfers molecular boundary information from mIF to overcome cytoplasmic contrast in H&E, establishing cross-modal supervision as a general strategy for recovering missing biological structure. We train VitaminP on 14 public datasets covering 34 cancer types and over 7 million instances, integrating publicly available labels with extensive annotations generated in this study, forming one of the largest resources for segmentation. VitaminP outperforms four state-of-the-art methods and generalizes to unseen datasets, including an in-house dataset spanning 24 rare cancer types. We further developed VitaminPScope, an open-source platform providing an interface for scalable inference and enabling broad adoption.

AI.tldr: VitaminP is a cross-modal learning framework that transfers molecular boundary information from multiplex immunofluorescence (mIF) to H&E images, enabling whole-cell segmentation from routine H&E staining. It outperforms existing methods across diverse datasets and is made accessible via the open-source VitaminPScope platform.
AI.motivation: Whole-cell and nuclear segmentation is essential for pathology but limited by H&E's poor cytoplasmic contrast. mIF enables precise cell delineation but is costly and inaccessible. The authors aim to recover missing cytoplasmic structure from H&E using cross-modal supervision from paired mIF data.
AI.method: VitaminP uses a cross-modal learning framework trained on paired H&E-mIF data, transferring molecular boundary information from mIF to H&E. It was trained on 14 public datasets (34 cancer types, >7M instances) with extensive annotations, integrating public labels and newly generated ones.
AI.result: VitaminP outperforms four state-of-the-art methods and generalizes well to unseen datasets, including an in-house dataset of 24 rare cancer types. An open-source platform, VitaminPScope, was also developed for scalable inference.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal supervision is a general strategy for recovering missing biological structural information from limited staining modalities, enabling accurate whole-cell segmentation from routine H&E images without the need for costly mIF.

title: Bringing a Personal Point of View: Evaluating Dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting for Egocentric Scene Reconstruction
comment: Accepted at the EgoVis Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Egocentric video provides a unique view into human perception and interaction, with growing relevance for augmented reality, robotics, and assistive technologies. However, rapid camera motion and complex scene dynamics pose major challenges for 3D reconstruction from this perspective. While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has become a state-of-the-art method for efficient, high-quality novel view synthesis, variants, that focus on reconstructing dynamic scenes from monocular video are rarely evaluated on egocentric video. It remains unclear whether existing models generalize to this setting or if egocentric-specific solutions are needed. In this work, we evaluate dynamic monocular 3DGS models on egocentric and exocentric video using paired ego-exo recordings from the EgoExo4D dataset. We find that reconstruction quality is consistently lower in egocentric views. Analysis reveals that the difference in reconstruction quality, measured in peak signal-to-noise ratio, stems from the reconstruction of static, not dynamic, content. Our findings underscore current limitations and motivate the development of egocentric-specific approaches, while also highlighting the value of separately evaluating static and dynamic regions of a video.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates dynamic monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting models on egocentric vs. exocentric video using EgoExo4D data, finding consistently lower reconstruction quality in egocentric views due to poorer reconstruction of static content, not dynamic content.
AI.motivation: Egocentric video is increasingly important for AR, robotics, and assistive tech, but rapid camera motion and complex dynamics make 3D reconstruction challenging. It's unclear if existing dynamic monocular 3DGS models generalize to egocentric video or if specialized solutions are needed.
AI.method: Evaluated dynamic monocular 3DGS models on paired ego-exo recordings from EgoExo4D dataset, comparing reconstruction quality (PSNR) across egocentric and exocentric views, and separately analyzing static vs. dynamic content.
AI.result: Reconstruction quality is consistently lower in egocentric views. The quality gap stems from poorer reconstruction of static content, not dynamic content.
AI.conclusion: Current dynamic 3DGS models have limitations on egocentric video, motivating development of egocentric-specific approaches. The paper also highlights the importance of separately evaluating static and dynamic regions in video reconstruction.

title: ShredBench: Evaluating the Semantic Reasoning Capabilities of Multimodal LLMs in Document Reconstruction
comment: ACL 2026 Findings. Code available at https://github.com/ythere-y/ShredBench
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance in Visually Rich Document Understanding (VRDU) tasks, but their capabilities are mainly evaluated on pristine, well-structured document images. We consider content restoration from shredded fragments, a challenging VRDU setting that requires integrating visual pattern recognition with semantic reasoning under significant content discontinuities. To facilitate systematic evaluation of complex VRDU tasks, we introduce ShredBench, a benchmark supported by an automated generation pipeline that renders fragmented documents directly from Markdown. The proposed pipeline ensures evaluation validity by allowing the flexible integration of latest or unseen textual sources to prevent training data contamination. ShredBench assesses four scenarios (English, Chinese, Code, Table) with three fragmentation granularities (8, 12, 16 pieces). Empirical evaluations on state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal a significant performance gap: The method is effective on intact documents; however, once the document is shredded, restoration becomes a significant challenge, with NED dropping sharply as fragmentation increases. Our findings highlight that current MLLMs lack the fine-grained cross-modal reasoning required to bridge visual discontinuities, identifying a critical gap in robust VRDU research.

AI.tldr: ShredBench is a new benchmark for evaluating MLLMs on content restoration from shredded document fragments, revealing that current models struggle significantly when document images are fragmented, with performance dropping sharply as fragmentation increases.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs are evaluated on pristine, well-structured document images. The authors aim to assess MLLM capabilities under challenging VRDU settings with content discontinuities, specifically content restoration from shredded fragments.
AI.method: ShredBench uses an automated generation pipeline that renders fragmented documents directly from Markdown, supporting four scenarios (English, Chinese, Code, Table) with three fragmentation granularities (8, 12, 16 pieces), while preventing training data contamination.
AI.result: State-of-the-art MLLMs show a significant performance gap: effective on intact documents but sharply declining NED scores as fragmentation increases. Current models lack fine-grained cross-modal reasoning for bridging visual discontinuities.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs lack the fine-grained cross-modal reasoning required to handle content discontinuities, identifying a critical gap in robust VRDU research.

title: Mapping License Plate Recoverability Under Extreme Viewing Angles for Oppor-tunistic Urban Sensing
summary: Urban environments contain many imaging sensors built for specific purposes, including ATM, body-worn, CCTV, and dashboard cameras. Under the opportunistic sensing paradigm, these sensors can be repurposed for secondary inference tasks such as license plate recognition. Yet objects of interest in such imagery are often noisy, low-resolution, and captured from extreme viewpoints. Recent advances in AI-based restoration can recover use-ful information even from severely degraded images. A central challenge is determining which distortion parame-ters allow reliable recovery and which lead to inference failure. This paper introduces recoverability maps, a task-agnostic method for quantifying this boundary. The method combines a dense synthetic sweep of degrada-tion parameters with two summary measures: boundary area-under-curve, which estimates the recoverable frac-tion of the parameter space, and a reliability score, which captures the frequency and severity of failures within that region. We demonstrate the method on license plate recognition from highly angled views under realistic camera artifacts. Several restoration architectures are trained and evaluated, including U-Net, Restormer, Pix2Pix, and SR3 diffusion. The best model recovers about 93% of the parameter space. Similar results across models sug-gest that sensing geometry, rather than architecture, sets the limit of recovery.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "recoverability maps" to quantify when degraded images can be reliably restored for secondary inference tasks, demonstrated on license plate recognition from extreme camera angles using multiple restoration architectures.
AI.motivation: Urban sensors (ATM, body-worn, CCTV cameras) can be repurposed for secondary tasks like license plate recognition, but images are often noisy, low-resolution, and from extreme viewpoints. A key challenge is determining which degradation levels still allow reliable recovery versus causing failure.
AI.method: The authors propose recoverability maps—a task-agnostic method combining dense synthetic sweeps of degradation parameters with two metrics: boundary area-under-curve (recoverable fraction of parameter space) and a reliability score (frequency/severity of failures). They test on license plate recognition from angled views using U-Net, Restormer, Pix2Pix, and SR3 diffusion models.
AI.result: The best model (SR3 diffusion) recovers ~93% of the parameter space. Similar performance across all architectures suggests sensing geometry (not model choice) limits recoverability.
AI.conclusion: Recoverability maps effectively quantify the boundary between recoverable and unrecoverable degradation. The finding that sensing geometry dominates recovery limits suggests future work should focus on sensor placement rather than architectural improvements alone.

title: Focus on What Matters: Two-Stage ROI-Aware Refinement for Anatomy-Preserving Fetal Ultrasound Reconstruction
comment: 18 pages, 7 figures, multiple tables. Preprint submitted to arXiv
summary: Measurement-critical ultrasound tasks often depend on a small anatomical region, making global reconstruction metrics an unreliable proxy for clinical fidelity. We propose an ROI-aware representation learning framework and instantiate it for first-trimester nuchal translucency (NT) screening under multi-hospital domain shift. A two-phase convolutional autoencoder (CAE) first learns a globally faithful 128-D latent code via MS-SSIM, then refines the NT ROI using intensity (L1) and normalized Sobel-edge constraints. To combine these heterogeneous objectives without manual tuning, we initialize loss weights via gradient-based calibration from per-term gradient magnitudes. Under strict hospital-wise evaluation with one hospital held out, ROI refinement improves both global and measurement-relevant quality: on the standard dev split it increases PSNR by +0.27 dB (val) and +0.29 dB (held-out test), reduces ROI MAE by 8.87% (val) and 6.43% (held-out test), and reduces ROI Edge-MAE by 11.10% on source hospitals and 4.90% on the unseen hospital. Beyond reconstruction, frozen-latent probes provide additional evidence of generalization: hospital provenance becomes less confidently predictable on the unseen site (0.556 to 0.541 max-softmax; 0.684 to 0.688 entropy) while OOD detection remains strong across site-held-out protocols (Mahalanobis AUROC up to 0.9956, with modest KNN gains in challenging splits). The same ROI-aware refinement principle is anatomy-agnostic and can be adopted for other fetal biometry targets (e.g., crown-rump length (CRL), nasal bone (NB)) and broader medical imaging settings where small ROIs dominate clinical decisions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an ROI-aware representation learning framework for NT screening that uses two-phase CAE training with MS-SSIM, L1, and Sobel-edge constraints to improve measurement-critical reconstruction quality under domain shift, achieving better PSNR, ROI MAE, and edge metrics while maintaining OOD detection performance.
AI.motivation: Global reconstruction metrics are unreliable proxies for clinical fidelity in ultrasound tasks where only a small anatomical region (ROI) matters for measurement-critical decisions, such as nuchal translucency (NT) screening.
AI.method: A two-phase convolutional autoencoder: (1) learns globally faithful 128-D latent code via MS-SSIM; (2) refines NT ROI using L1 intensity and normalized Sobel-edge constraints. Loss weights are initialized via gradient-based calibration from per-term gradient magnitudes to avoid manual tuning.
AI.result: Under hospital-wise evaluation, ROI refinement improves PSNR by +0.27 dB (val) and +0.29 dB (held-out test), reduces ROI MAE by 8.87% (val) and 6.43% (held-out test), and reduces ROI Edge-MAE by 11.10% (source) and 4.90% (unseen). Frozen-latent probes show reduced confident hospital classification on unseen site while OOD detection remains strong (Mahalanobis AUROC up to 0.9956).
AI.conclusion: The ROI-aware refinement principle is anatomy-agnostic and can be applied to other fetal biometry targets (e.g., CRL, nasal bone) and broader medical imaging settings where small ROIs dominate clinical decisions.

title: Latent Inter-Frame Pruning: A Training-Free Method Bridging Traditional Video Compression and Modern Diffusion Transformers for Efficient Generation
summary: Video generation, while capable of generating realistic videos, is computationally expensive and slow, prohibiting real-time applications. In this paper, we observe that video latents encoded via an autoencoder under the Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) framework contain redundancy along the temporal axis. Analogous to how traditional video compression algorithms avoid transmitting redundant frame data, we propose the Latent Inter-frame Pruning framework to prune (skip the re-computation of) duplicated latent patches, thereby reducing computational burden and increasing throughput. However, direct pruning results in visual artifacts due to the discrepancy between full-sequence training and pruned inference. To resolve these artifacts, we propose an Attention Recovery mechanism to bridge the train-inference gap. With our proposed method, we increase video editing throughput by 1.44$\times$, achieving 12.44 FPS on an NVIDIA RTX 6000 while maintaining video quality. We hope our work inspires further research into integrating traditional video compression methods with modern video generation pipelines. This work is a preliminary work on Training-free Latent Inter-Frame Pruning with Attention Recovery.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Latent Inter-frame Pruning to skip redundant latent patches in LDM-based video generation, plus Attention Recovery to fix train-inference gap artifacts, achieving 1.44× throughput increase (12.44 FPS) on RTX 6000 for video editing.
AI.motivation: Video generation under LDM is computationally expensive and slow, hindering real-time use. The authors observe temporal redundancy in video latents, similar to traditional video compression, and aim to reduce computation by pruning duplicated patches.
AI.method: 1) Latent Inter-frame Pruning: skip re-computation of temporally redundant latent patches during inference. 2) Attention Recovery mechanism: resolves visual artifacts caused by the train-inference gap between full-sequence training and pruned inference.
AI.result: 1.44× throughput increase for video editing, achieving 12.44 FPS on NVIDIA RTX 6000 while maintaining video quality.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a training-free method inspired by traditional video compression to accelerate LDM-based video generation, and hopes to inspire further integration between compression methods and modern generation pipelines.

title: Exploring Audio Hallucination in Egocentric Video Understanding
summary: Egocentric videos provide a distinctive setting in which sound serves as crucial cues to understand user activities and surroundings, particularly when visual information is unstable or occluded due to continuous camera movement. State-of-the-art large audio-visual language models (AV-LLMs) can generate multimodal descriptions. However, we show in this work that they are prone to audio hallucinations, often inferring sounds from visual cues that are visible but not heard. We present a systematic and automatic evaluation framework for analyzing audio hallucinations in egocentric video through a targeted question-answering (Q/A) protocol. We curate a dataset of 300 egocentric videos and design 1,000 sound-focused questions to probe model outputs. To characterize hallucinations, we propose a grounded taxonomy that distinguishes between foreground action sounds from the user activities and background ambient sounds. Our evaluation shows that advanced AV-LLMs, such as Qwen2.5 Omni, exhibit high hallucination rates, achieving only 27.3% and 39.5% accuracy on Q/As related to foreground and background sounds, respectively. With this work, we highlight the need to measure the reliability of multimodal responses, emphasizing that robust evaluation of hallucinations is essential to develop reliable AV-LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that audio-visual LLMs suffer from audio hallucinations in egocentric videos—inferring sounds from visual cues that aren't actually heard. The authors create a 300-video dataset with 1,000 sound-focused Q/As and a taxonomy distinguishing foreground vs. background sounds. Their evaluation shows high hallucination rates (e.g., Qwen2.5 Omni achieves only 27.3% and 39.5% accuracy on foreground and background questions), highlighting the need for robust hallucination evaluation in AV-LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current AV-LLMs tend to hallucinate audio content—inferring sounds from visual information that are visible but not actually audible. This is especially problematic in egocentric videos where visual input is often unstable or occluded, making sound a critical modality. The lack of systematic evaluation for such audio hallucinations motivated the authors to develop a rigorous framework.
AI.method: The authors create a dataset of 300 egocentric videos with 1,000 targeted sound-focused Q/A pairs. They propose a grounded taxonomy that categorizes audio hallucinations into foreground action sounds (from user activities) and background ambient sounds. They then evaluate state-of-the-art AV-LLMs like Qwen2.5 Omni using this framework.
AI.result: Advanced AV-LLMs exhibit high hallucination rates: Qwen2.5 Omni achieves only 27.3% accuracy on foreground sound Q/As and 39.5% accuracy on background sound Q/As.
AI.conclusion: The work highlights the critical need for robust evaluation of audio hallucinations in multimodal models. Measuring reliability of multimodal responses is essential for developing trustworthy AV-LLMs, especially in egocentric video settings.

title: Empirical Ablation and Ensemble Optimization of a Convolutional Neural Network for CIFAR-10 Classification
summary: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) remain a central approach in image classification, but their performance depends strongly on architectural and training choices. This paper presents an empirical ablation-based study of CNN optimization for the CIFAR-10 benchmark. The study evaluates 17 progressive modifications involving training duration, learning-rate scheduling, dropout configuration, pooling strategy, network depth, filter arrangement, and dense-layer design. The goal is to identify which changes improve generalization and which increase complexity without improving performance. The baseline model achieved 79.5\% test accuracy. Extending training duration improved performance steadily, whereas several structural redesigns reduced accuracy despite greater architectural variation. Based on the strongest individual configurations, a weighted ensemble was constructed, achieving 86.38\% accuracy in the reduced-data setting and 89.23\% when trained using the full CIFAR-10 dataset. These results suggest that performance gains in CNN-based classification depend less on indiscriminate increases in depth or parameter count than on careful empirical selection of training and architectural modifications. The study therefore highlights the practical value of ablation-oriented optimization and ensemble learning for small-image classification.

AI.tldr: This paper conducts an empirical ablation study on CNN optimization for CIFAR-10, testing 17 progressive modifications. The best configurations combined into a weighted ensemble achieve 86.38% (reduced data) and 89.23% (full data) accuracy, showing that careful empirical tuning outperforms indiscriminate architectural expansion.
AI.motivation: To systematically identify which architectural and training modifications actually improve CNN generalization for image classification, and which merely increase complexity without benefit.
AI.method: An ablation-based study evaluating 17 progressive modifications on a CNN baseline (79.5% accuracy), including training duration, learning-rate scheduling, dropout, pooling, depth, filter arrangement, and dense-layer design, concluding with a weighted ensemble of the best configurations.
AI.result: Extended training duration steadily improved performance; several structural redesigns reduced accuracy. Weighted ensemble achieved 86.38% (reduced data) and 89.23% (full CIFAR-10) accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Performance gains depend more on careful empirical selection of modifications than on indiscriminate increases in depth or parameter count, highlighting the value of ablation-based optimization and ensemble learning.

title: Risk-Aware Robust Learning: Reducing Clinical Risk under Label Noise in Medical Image Classification
summary: Noisy labels are a pervasive challenge in medical image classification, where annotation errors arise from inter-observer variability and diagnostic ambiguity. Although several noise-robust learning methods have been proposed, their evaluation predominantly relies on accuracy-oriented metrics, overlooking the clinical implications of asymmetric error costs. In medical diagnosis, a false negative (missed disease) carries substantially higher consequences than a false positive (false alarm), as delayed treatment can directly impact patient outcomes. In this work, we investigate whether noise-robust training methods preserve clinical safety under label noise. We conduct a systematic risk-aware evaluation of the state-of-the-art noise-robust methods Coteaching, DivideMix, UNICON, and a GMM-based filtering approach on binarized DermaMNIST and PathMNIST datasets under clean and label noise rates of 20%, and 40%. Beyond balanced accuracy, we adopt a cost-sensitive Global Risk formulation that explicitly penalizes false negatives. Our analysis reveals that the robustness of state-of-the-art methods does not guarantee clinical safety. Furthermore, we demonstrate that integrating cost-sensitive optimization into noise-robust training significantly reduces clinical risk, while mantaining model utility. These findings demonstrate that noise-robust learning must be evaluated through a clinical risk lens, and that combining robust training with cost-sensitive optimization can meaningfully reduce risk in noisy-label medical imaging scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates noise-robust learning methods for medical image classification under a clinical risk lens, finding that standard robust methods don't guarantee clinical safety (especially regarding false negatives), and showing that integrating cost-sensitive optimization reduces clinical risk while maintaining utility.
AI.motivation: Current noise-robust learning methods are evaluated using accuracy-oriented metrics, ignoring the asymmetric costs of errors in medical diagnosis where false negatives (missed disease) are far more harmful than false positives.
AI.method: Systematic risk-aware evaluation of Coteaching, DivideMix, UNICON, and GMM-based filtering on binarized DermaMNIST and PathMNIST under clean and noisy (20%, 40%) conditions, using both balanced accuracy and a cost-sensitive Global Risk formulation that penalizes false negatives.
AI.result: State-of-the-art noise-robust methods do not guarantee clinical safety; integrating cost-sensitive optimization significantly reduces clinical risk while maintaining model utility.
AI.conclusion: Noise-robust learning must be evaluated through a clinical risk lens, and combining robust training with cost-sensitive optimization can meaningfully reduce risk in noisy-label medical imaging scenarios.

title: Mammographic Lesion Segmentation with Lightweight Models: A Comparative Study
summary: Breast cancer is a leading cause of cancer-related mortality among women worldwide, with mammography as the primary screening tool. While deep learning models have shown strong performance in lesion segmentation, most rely on computationally intensive architectures that limit their use in resource-constrained environments. This study evaluates the performance and efficiency of lightweight models for mammographic lesion segmentation. Architectures including MobileNetV2, EfficientNet Lite, ENet, and Fast-SCNN were compared against a U-Net baseline using the INbreast dataset with 5-fold cross-validation. Performance was assessed using Dice score, Intersection over Union (IoU), and Recall, alongside model complexity. MobileNetV2 with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SCSE) achieved the best performance, with a Dice score of 0.5766 while using approximately 75\% fewer parameters than U-Net. Cross-dataset evaluation on the DMID dataset showed reduced accuracy due to domain shift but preserved recall. These results demonstrate that lightweight architectures offer a practical balance between performance and efficiency for deployable CAD systems.

AI.tldr: Lightweight deep learning models (especially MobileNetV2 with SCSE) can achieve competitive mammographic lesion segmentation with ~75% fewer parameters than U-Net, offering a practical trade-off for deployable CAD systems.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning models for mammographic lesion segmentation are computationally intensive, limiting deployment in resource-constrained environments. This study explores lightweight alternatives to enable practical CAD systems.
AI.method: Compared lightweight architectures (MobileNetV2, EfficientNet Lite, ENet, Fast-SCNN) against U-Net baseline on INbreast dataset using 5-fold cross-validation. Evaluated with Dice, IoU, Recall, and model complexity metrics. Cross-dataset evaluation performed on DMID.
AI.result: MobileNetV2 with SCSE achieved best Dice score of 0.5766 with ~75% fewer parameters than U-Net. Cross-dataset evaluation on DMID showed reduced accuracy due to domain shift but preserved recall.
